text: Spatial molecular profiling has provided biomedical researchers valuable opportunities to better understand the relationship between cellular localization and tissue function. Effectively modeling multimodal spatial omics data is crucial for understanding tissue complexity and underlying biology. Furthermore, improvements in spatial resolution have led to the advent of technologies that can generate spatial molecular data with subcellular resolution, requiring the development of computationally efficient methods that can handle the resulting large-scale datasets. MISO (MultI-modal Spatial Omics) is a versatile algorithm for feature extraction and clustering, capable of integrating multiple modalities from diverse spatial omics experiments with high spatial resolution. Its effectiveness is demonstrated across various datasets, encompassing gene expression, protein expression, epigenetics, metabolomics and tissue histology modalities. MISO outperforms existing methods in identifying biologically relevant spatial domains, representing a substantial advancement in multimodal spatial omics analysis. Moreover, MISO's computational efficiency ensures its scalability to handle large-scale datasets generated by subcellular resolution spatial omics technologies.

Multimodal spatial omics represents a paradigm shift in understanding complex biological systems by integrating diverse omics modalities within their original tissue contexts. This approach provides a comprehensive view of cellular and tissue functions, crucial for unraveling the molecular mechanisms of diseases. Recent technologies include spatial transcriptomics, spatial epigenome-transcriptome co-profiling, spatial gene and protein expression, and spatial transcriptomics and metabolomics. A critical step is amalgamating features from different modalities to create cohesive low-dimensional data representations for downstream analyses like spatial domain detection. Current methods, however, either ignore spatial information or are limited to specific modality pairs and require extensive hyperparameter tuning. MISO (MultI-modal Spatial Omics) is a feature extraction and spatial clustering algorithm designed for comprehensive modality integration, including both omics and tissue histology imaging data. MISO excels in accurately integrating different modalities and segregating spots into distinct, biologically relevant spatial domains. A notable advantage is its minimal requirement for hyperparameter tuning and its ability to mitigate artifacts associated with low-quality modalities.

The MISO workflow begins with the extraction of low-dimensional embeddings for each modality using multilayer perceptrons, which are trained to minimize spectral clustering and reconstruction loss functions. MISO then calculates the outer product between pairs of modality-specific embeddings to construct features that capture the interactions between modalities. These modality-specific and interaction feature vectors are concatenated to produce comprehensive embeddings that encapsulate all modalities. The refined subset of embeddings is then used as input for a k-means clustering algorithm to separate the spots into distinct clusters, each reflective of the unique characteristics defined by the input modalities. MISO is computationally fast, processing medium-scale (1,000-10,000 spots/cells) datasets in under 1 minute on a single GPU and scales effectively to large datasets (>10,000 spots/cells).

Applied to a bladder cancer 10x Visium V2 dataset with spatial transcriptomics and histology data, MISO aimed to identify fine-grained structures like tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) and high endothelial venules (HEVs). Since Visium spots (55μm) are larger than HEVs (~40μm), resolution enhancement via iStar was used to generate 4x4μm² super-pixels. MISO accurately localized clusters to HEVs with high sensitivity and specificity (few false positives), outperforming SpatialGlue (high sensitivity but low specificity) and MUSE (failed to produce relevant clusters). Quantitative evaluation using Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) showed MISO's superior performance in clustering based on both RNA and tissue histology data.

On a gastric cancer 10x Xenium dataset (377 genes, ~700k cells), MISO successfully distinguished poorly cohesive carcinoma from discohesive tumor cells, mucosa, and muscle, accurately localizing lymphoid aggregates. This demonstrates MISO's ability to handle large-scale data and integrate histology image features to differentiate regions with similar marker gene expression (e.g., ERBB2 in carcinoma vs. mucosa) but distinct histological features. Neither MUSE nor SpatialGlue could analyze this dataset due to memory constraints.

Analyzing a colorectal cancer (CRC) 10x Visium HD dataset (subcellular 8x8μm² bins), MISO identified subclasses of invasive carcinoma. Notably, one cluster (cluster 4) colocalized with SPPI+ macrophages (associated with CRC progression), while other cancerous regions did not show this extent of SPPI colocalization. MISO differentiated this potentially more aggressive subcluster based on its proximity to SPPI+ macrophages, leveraging hierarchical tissue histology image features capturing short-range and long-range dependencies. MUSE and SpatialGlue were again limited by memory constraints.

section: Results - Application to Mouse Embryo (Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq)
text: Applied to a mouse embryo (E13) spatial ATAC-RNA-seq dataset, MISO uniquely distinguished the ventricular zone (VZ) from the pallium stratum in the telencephalon, validated by marker gene expression (SOX2 for VZ, TBR1 for pallium). It also identified the subpallium stratum via GAD2 expression. Visualization in t-SNE plots showed MISO clusters (VZ vs. pallium stratum) had almost complete separation for both RNA and ATAC modalities, outperforming MUSE and SpatialGlue.

MISO analyzed a mouse hippocampus dataset with spatial transcriptomics (10x Visium), spatial metabolomics (MALDI-MSI), and H&E imaging. After resolution enhancement via iStar, MISO accurately localized clusters to structural components like the pyramidal layer subfields (CA1sp, CA2sp, CA3sp). While other methods differentiated CA1sp from CA3sp, MISO was the only one accurately localizing the much smaller CA2sp subfield, validated against the MERFISH whole mouse brain atlas.

section: Results - Application to Tonsil (Visium Gene & Protein)
text: On a human tonsil spatial gene and protein expression dataset (35 proteins), MISO was evaluated on localizing germinal centers. Using all three modalities (RNA, protein, histology) or any two-modality combination, MISO consistently outperformed MUSE and SpatialGlue in F1 score for germinal center localization. The maximum F1 score (0.90) was achieved by MISO with all three modalities, demonstrating its robustness regardless of the number and type of modalities involved.

MISO handles raw data preprocessing (count matrices, log-normalization) and accepts raw H&E images, extracting features via a hierarchical Vision Transformer (ViT) model pretrained on whole-slide images. This yields 576-dimensional embeddings per spot capturing local and global image structures. For omics data, MISO constructs an adjacency matrix $A^{m}$ for modality $m$: $A^{m}_{ij} = \exp\left(\frac{-\left\|x_{i}^{m}-x_{j}^{m}\right\|_{2}^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$, where $\sigma^{2}=30$. Spectral clustering-based autoencoders extract 32-dimensional embeddings $y_{i}^{m}$ per modality, minimizing spectral clustering loss $L_{SC}^{m}=\sum_{i, j} A_{i, j}^{m}\left\|y_{i}^{m}-y_{j}^{m}\right\|_{2}^{2}$ and reconstruction loss $L_{R}^{m}=\sum_{i}\left\|z_{i}^{m}-\hat{z}_{i}^{m}\right\|_{2}^{2}$. Cross-modality feature fusion calculates interaction features $y_{i}^{m_{1}\otimes m_{2}} = y_{i}^{m_{1}} \otimes y_{i}^{m_{2}}$ (outer product), flattened and combined via PCA (top 32 PCs) before concatenation with modality-specific vectors for k-means input.

To scale to large datasets (e.g., >100k spots), MISO modifies the adjacency matrix calculation using a k-nearest neighbors approach ($k=100$), storing it as a sparse matrix. For quality control, users perform a binary determination on whether to include modality-specific features based on visual inspection (histology artifacts), total UMI counts/genes detected (transcriptomics), spatial localization of metabolites (metabolomics), or protein expression variation (proteomics). MISO simplifies integration by relying on this user-defined quality filter rather than automated detection.

MISO presents a versatile algorithm capable of accommodating all modalities in multimodal spatial omics experiments, distinct from existing methods in its flexibility, robustness to low-quality modalities, and minimal user-dependent parameter tuning. Demonstrated across 16 datasets (brain, embryo, colon in mice; melanoma in zebrafish; bladder, gastric, breast, prostate cancer, tonsil in humans), MISO consistently outperformed MUSE and SpatialGlue in identifying known biological structures. Limitations include its current specificity to H&E-stained images and its unsupervised nature requiring user interpretation for biological annotation. Future plans include extending feature extraction to other imaging types and enhancing functionality for simultaneous multi-sample analysis.
